questions.text:
### Summary of Technical Compendium: Mathematical Foundations and Performance Metrics

**1. Network Architecture Foundations**
Network effectiveness is measured by four core pillars:
- **Delivery**: Data must reach only the intended destination.
- **Accuracy**: Data must be uncorrupted; effectiveness is inversely proportional to error rate.
- **Timeliness**: Delivery within acceptable timeframes (critical for VoIP/Live data).
- **Jitter**: Variation in packet arrival times, leading to buffer requirements or stale data.

**2. Topographical Mathematics (Mesh Topology)**
The number of required links for \( n \) stations is calculated as:
- **Simplex/Half-Duplex**: \( n(n-1) \)
- **Full-Duplex**: \[ \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \]
| Stations (n) | Links (Full-Duplex) |
|---|---|
| 2 | 1 |
| 3 | 3 |
| 4 | 6 |
| 5 | 10 |
| 10 | 45 |

**3. Performance Metrics**
- **Throughput**: \[ \text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Successfully delivered packets}}{\text{Unit of time (seconds)}} \]
- **Jitter Calculation**: The absolute difference between sequential arrival intervals (e.g., \( |P_2 - P_1| \)). High jitter forces high memory/buffer usage.

**4. Physics and Units**
- **Attenuation vs. Gauge**: 
  - Gauge (G) \( \uparrow \) \( \implies \) Diameter (D) \( \downarrow \)
  - Gauge (G) \( \uparrow \) \( \implies \) Attenuation \( \uparrow \)
- **Unit Multipliers**:
  - **Data (K/M/G/T)**: \( 10^3, 10^6, 10^9, 10^{12} \)
  - **Temporal (m/\mu/n)**: \( 10^{-3}, 10^{-6}, 10^{-9} \)

**5. Delay Modeling**
- **Transmission Time (TT)**: \( \frac{\text{Message Size}}{\text{Bandwidth}} \)
- **Propagation Time (PT)**: \( \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Speed}} \)
- **Total Delay (TD)** formulas (where \( k \) is hops/switches+1):
  - **Circuit-Switched**: \( TD = PT \times 4 + TT_{S\&T} \times 3 + TT_D \) (4 phases: Setup, Request, Response, Data).
  - **Datagram**: \( TD = PT_{A \to B} + TT \times 3 + WT_{R1} + WT_{R2} \) (Includes Store-and-Forward delay and Waiting Time).
  - **Virtual-Circuit**: \( TD = PT_{A \to B} \times 4 + TT_{S\&T} \times (3 \times 3) + TT_D \times 3 \).

**6. Architectural Differences**
- **Circuit Switching**: Continuous flow, path-oriented, minimal nodal delay after setup.
- **Packet Switching (Datagram/VC)**: Resource efficient but utilizes **Store and Forward** and introduces unpredictable queuing delays (WT).

choices.label: A
choices.text: i have readd all the above

questions.explanation: This summary covers all laws, mathematical formulas, and networking concepts presented in the text, including the Mesh topology formulas, delay calculations for different switching methods, and the relationship between wire gauge and attenuation.

questions.text: A network delivers 12,000 packets in 6 seconds. During transmission, packet delays are recorded as 1.2 s, 1.5 s, 1.1 s, and 1.9 s. Calculate the network throughput and the jitter values between consecutive packets.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Throughput: 2,000 packets/s; Jitter: 0.3 s, 0.4 s, 0.8 s

choices.label: B
choices.text: Throughput: 1,200 packets/s; Jitter: 0.2 s, 0.3 s, 0.7 s

choices.label: C
choices.text: Throughput: 2,000 packets/s; Jitter: 0.1 s, 0.2 s, 0.3 s

choices.label: D
choices.text: Throughput: 6,000 packets/s; Jitter: 0.5 s, 0.5 s, 0.5 s

questions.explanation: Throughput = Total Packets / Total Time = \( 12,000 / 6 = 2,000 \) packets/s. Jitter is the absolute difference between consecutive delays: \( |1.5 - 1.2| = 0.3 \), \( |1.1 - 1.5| = 0.4 \), and \( |1.9 - 1.1| = 0.8 \).

questions.text: A company plans to build a full-duplex mesh network with 8 stations. How many physical links are required?
questions.explanation: The number of links in a mesh topology is given by \( n(n-1)/2 \). For 8 stations: \( 8(7)/2 = 28 \). Duplex type refers to the capacity, but the physical link count remains the same.

questions.text: A server successfully transmits 900 MB of data in 30 seconds. What is the throughput in Mbps?
questions.explanation: 900 MB = \( 900 \times 8 = 7200 \) Mbits. Throughput = \( 7200 \text{ Mbits} / 30 \text{ s} = 240 \) Mbps.

questions.text: Four packets arrive at times: 2 ms, 5 ms, 3 ms, and 9 ms. Compute the jitter values between each consecutive pair and determine the maximum jitter.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Jitters: 3 ms, 2 ms, 6 ms; Max Jitter: 6 ms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Jitters: 1 ms, 2 ms, 3 ms; Max Jitter: 3 ms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Jitters: 3 ms, 3 ms, 3 ms; Max Jitter: 3 ms

choices.label: D
choices.text: Jitters: 2 ms, 5 ms, 6 ms; Max Jitter: 6 ms

questions.text: Two cables are used: Cable A (AWG 18) and Cable B (AWG 26). Which cable will experience higher attenuation, and why?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cable A; because lower AWG means a thinner wire with more resistance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cable B; because higher AWG means a thinner wire with higher resistance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cable A; because higher AWG means a thicker wire.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both experience the same attenuation regardless of gauge.

questions.explanation: Higher American Wire Gauge (AWG) numbers represent thinner wires. Thinner wires have higher electrical resistance, which leads to higher signal loss (attenuation) over distance.

questions.text: A link transmits data at 2 Gbps, and a packet takes 3 microseconds to arrive. Calculate how many bits are transmitted during that time.
questions.explanation: 2 Gbps = \( 2 \times 10^9 \) bps. 3 \( \mu s \) = \( 3 \times 10^{-6} \) s. Bits = \( (2 \times 10^9) \times (3 \times 10^{-6}) = 6,000 \) bits.

questions.text: A message of 5000 bits is sent over a link with bandwidth 2 Mbps. What is the transmission time (TT)?
questions.explanation: TT = Packet Size / Bandwidth = \( 5000 / (2 \times 10^6) = 0.0025 \) seconds = 2.5 ms.

questions.text: A signal travels 15 km over a medium with propagation speed \( 2.5 \times 10^8 \) m/s. Calculate the propagation time (PT).
A packet of 1000 bits travels over a 20 km link with bandwidth 5 Mbps and propagation speed \( 2 \times 10^8 \) m/s. Find the total delay (TD = TT + PT).
In a circuit-switched network: PT = 2 ms, TT (setup & teardown) = 1 ms, TT (data) = 5 ms. Calculate total delay.
questions.explanation: For circuit switching: Total Delay = Setup + PT + Data TT = \( 1 + 2 + 5 = 8 \) ms. (Note: PT is usually applied per phase, but based on simple summation provided: 8ms).

questions.text: A packet travels through 2 routers: PT = 3 ms, TT = 2 ms per hop, Waiting times: R1 = 4 ms, R2 = 6 ms. Compute the total delay.
questions.explanation: Total hops = 3. Total TT = \( 3 \times 2 = 6 \). Total Delay = Total TT + PT + Queueing = \( 6 + 3 + (4 + 6) = 19 \) ms.

questions.text: Given a Virtual-Circuit network: PT = 1 ms, TT (setup/teardown) = 2 ms, TT (data) = 3 ms. Find the total delay.
questions.explanation: Total Delay = Setup + PT + Data TT = \( 2 + 1 + 3 = 6 \) ms.

questions.text: A network transmits 18,000 packets in 9 seconds, but 10% are corrupted and discarded. Packet arrival delays are: 1.0 s, 1.8 s, 1.2 s, 2.5 s, 1.7 s. Calculate the effective throughput and average jitter.
questions.explanation: Successful packets = \( 18,000 - 10\% = 16,200 \). Effective Throughput = \( 16,200 / 9 = 1,800 \) but often specified as total throughput reduction which is 1,620 (1800 * 0.9). Jitters: 0.8, 0.6, 1.3, 0.8. Avg = \( (0.8+0.6+1.3+0.8)/4 = 0.875 \). Re-calculating based on standard context: Effective Throughput is 1,800 successful packets/s. Option D is intended given the packet count logic.

questions.text: A full-duplex mesh network requires 66 links. How many stations are in the network, and how many total links are needed if redundant parallel links are added between each pair?
questions.explanation: \( n(n-1)/2 = 66 \implies n^2 - n - 132 = 0 \implies (n-12)(n+11)=0 \). Stations = 12. Redundant links (2 per pair) = \( 66 \times 2 = 132 \).

questions.text: A file of size 120 MB is transmitted in 40 seconds, but 25% of the bandwidth is consumed by protocol overhead. What is the effective throughput in Mbps?
questions.explanation: Total bits = \( 120 \times 8 = 960 \) Mb. Total Throughput = \( 960 / 40 = 24 \) Mbps. Effective (75%) = \( 24 \times 0.75 = 18 \) Mbps.

questions.text: Packet arrival times (in ms): 3, 9, 4, 10, 6. Identify the maximum jitter value.
Cable A (AWG 20, 100m max) and Cable B (AWG 28, 60m max) must cover 180m. How many segments (repeaters) are needed for each?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cable A: 2 segments (1 repeater); Cable B: 3 segments (2 repeaters)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cable A: 3 segments (2 repeaters); Cable B: 4 segments (3 repeaters)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cable A: 1 segment; Cable B: 2 segments

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cable A: 2 segments (2 repeaters); Cable B: 3 segments (3 repeaters)

questions.explanation: Cable A: \( 180/100 = 1.8 \to 2 \) segments (1 repeater). Cable B: \( 180/60 = 3 \) segments (2 repeaters).

questions.text: A system transmits at 0.5 Gbps for 250 microseconds. Calculate the total data transmitted in kilobytes (KB).
A 12,000-bit message is split into 3 equal packets, each sent over a 3 Mbps link. What is the total transmission time assuming packets are sent sequentially?
questions.explanation: Sequential TT = Total Size / Bandwidth = \( 12,000 / (3 \times 10^6) = 0.004 \) s = 4 ms. (Alternatively: \( 4000 / 3M = 1.33 \text{ ms per packet} \times 3 = 4 \text{ ms} \)).

questions.text: A signal travels 8 km at \( 2 \times 10^8 \) m/s then 4 km at \( 1.5 \times 10^8 \) m/s. What is the total propagation time?
A packet of 2000 bits travels across Link 1 (10 km, 4 Mbps) and Link 2 (20 km, 2 Mbps) with propagation speed \( 2 \times 10^8 \) m/s. Calculate the end-to-end delay.
Given PT = 1 ms, Setup = 4 ms, Data TT = 2 ms in circuit switching. What percentage of total delay is due to setup?
A packet crosses 3 routers: PT = 2 ms, TT = 1 ms/hop, Waiting times: 3 ms, 7 ms, 5 ms. Which component contributes most to delay?
questions.explanation: Total Wait = \( 3+7+5 = 15 \) ms. Total TT = \( 3 \times 1 = 3 \) ms. PT = 2 ms. Waiting time is the dominant factor.

questions.text: Given PT = 2 ms, VC Setup = 1 ms, VC Data TT = 4 ms. A Datagram network has same PT/TT but no setup and 6 ms total waiting time. Which is faster?
questions.explanation: VC Delay = \( 1 + 2 + 4 = 7 \) ms. Datagram Delay = \( 0 \text{ (setup)} + 2 \text{ (PT)} + 4 \text{ (TT)} + 6 \text{ (Wait)} = 12 \) ms. VC is faster.

questions.text: A network transmits 24,000 packets over 12 seconds. For the first 4 seconds, no packets are delivered. 15% of remaining packets are lost. What is the effective throughput over the full 12 seconds?
questions.explanation: Effective Throughput is based on successful delivery. Out of 24,000, 15% are lost (assuming 24,000 was the attempt). Successful = \( 24,000 \times 0.85 = 20,400 \). Throughput = \( 20,400 / 12 = 1,700 \) packets/s.

questions.text: A network grows from 6 to 12 stations (full-duplex mesh). By what factor did the number of links increase?
A link has 10 Mbps bandwidth. Utilization is 60%, and 20% of transmitted packets are retransmissions. What is the useful throughput?
questions.explanation: Actual transmission rate = \( 10 \times 0.6 = 6 \) Mbps. Useful data = \( 80\% \text{ of } 6 = 4.8 \) Mbps.

questions.text: Packet arrival timestamps (ms): 0, 4, 9, 15, 22. What are the jitter values?
questions.explanation: Inter-arrival times: 4, 5, 6, 7. Jitters: \( |5-4|=1, |6-5|=1, |7-6|=1 \). (Calculated from inter-arrival variance).

questions.text: You must design a 300 m link. AWG 22 (100 m max) or AWG 26 (70 m max). Each repeater adds 2 \( \mu s \) delay. Which design is better if latency is critical?
questions.explanation: AWG 22: \( 300/100 = 3 \) segments (2 repeaters). AWG 26: \( 300/70 = 4.28 \to 5 \) segments (4 repeaters). AWG 22 has less delay.

questions.text: A system transmits 1.5 Gb of data in 750 \( \mu s \). Calculate the data rate in Gbps.
questions.explanation: Rate = \( 1.5 \times 10^9 \text{ bits} / 750 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2 \times 10^{12} \text{ bps} = 2,000 \) Gbps.

questions.text: A 9000-bit message is split into 3 packets and sent over 3 hops (3 Mbps each). Compute total time if pipelining is applied.
questions.explanation: Each packet TT = \( 3000 / 3M = 1 \) ms. Total time = \( (\text{hops} + \text{packets} - 1) \times TT = (3 + 3 - 1) \times 1 = 5 \) ms.

questions.text: A 1-bit message is sent 5000 km at \( 2 \times 10^8 \) m/s over 1 Gbps bandwidth. Which component dominates the delay?
questions.explanation: TT = \( 1 / 10^9 = 1 \) ns. PT = \( 5 \times 10^6 / 2 \times 10^8 = 25 \) ms. PT dominates by a factor of 25 million.

questions.text: A packet (4000 bits) travels across 3 links: L1 (5M, 5km), L2 (1M, 10km), L3 (5M, 5km). What is the total TT?
questions.explanation: TT1 = 0.8 ms, TT2 = 4.0 ms, TT3 = 0.8 ms. Total TT = 5.6 ms.

questions.text: In circuit switching, PT = 2 ms and Setup/Teardown = 3 ms. At what data size (bits) does data transmission delay (at 1 Mbps) equal setup delay?
questions.explanation: Setup cost = 3 ms. Data TT = Size / Bandwidth. \( 3 \times 10^{-3} = \text{Size} / 10^6 \implies \text{Size} = 3000 \) bits.

questions.text: A datagram packet crosses 2 routers: PT = 1 ms/link, TT = 2 ms/hop, Wait = 0-10 ms/router. What is the maximum possible delay?
questions.explanation: Total hops = 3. Max Delay = \( (3 \times TT) + (3 \times PT) + (2 \times \text{Max Wait}) = (3 \times 2) + (3 \times 1) + (2 \times 10) = 6 + 3 + 20 = 29 \) ms.

questions.text: VC Setup = 12 ms. Datagram has 3 ms waiting per hop (3 hops). Ignoring PT and TT differences, at how many packets (n) does Virtual Circuit become more efficient?
questions.explanation: Datagram overhead per packet = 9 ms. VC overhead = 12 ms (one time). \( 12 < 9n \). For \( n=1 \), Datagram is 9, VC is 12. For \( n=2 \), Datagram is 18, VC is 12. Thus \( n > 1.33 \), so 2 packets.
